It takes a disciplined approach, which is something I've learned running my own electrical work; you can't just wire up a circuit hoping it'll work. You have to check your connections. Dealing with fake stuff isn't about getting angry at every post, because that's just letting the negativity short-circuit your peace, and that's not how I want to operate. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When you're just checking in on social media a few times a month, like I do, you don't want to get bogged down in the noise, and fake content is just a lot of noise. For a guy like me, who likes things to be solid and real, like a good piece of equipment or a solid match of football, this whole digital fluff can be frustrating. It takes a disciplined approach, which is something I've learned running my own electrical work; you can't just wire up a circuit hoping it'll work. You have to check your connections. Dealing with fake stuff isn't about getting angry at every post, because that's just letting the negativity short-circuit your peace, and that's not how I want to operate. It’s about applying a critical, almost methodical filter. I look at the source first—who is putting this out there? Are they someone I trust, someone whose opinions I value, or is it just some random account flashing bright lights? If it seems too wild, too perfect, or too inflammatory, I treat it like a faulty wire; I don't connect it to my main system. Furthermore, I try to look for corroboration. If a big story is going around, I don't just take the headline from some random feed; I'll see if a couple of established, reliable sources—maybe even checking local news before I jump on it—are saying the same thing. It’s that sense of thoroughness, that high conscientiousness, that pushes you to verify before you accept. Sometimes, I even think about the creator’s intent. Is this meant to inform, or is it meant to provoke a reaction? From a Christian perspective, I believe in seeking truth, and fake content is a distortion of that truth. So, my strategy is simple: slow down the scroll, check the connection, and only accept what has been properly grounded in reality.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is conscientious, agreeable, and low in extraversion, suggesting a thoughtful, reserved, and methodical approach. As a self-employed electrician, practical, reliable solutions are preferred. The tone should be steady, thoughtful, and grounded, reflecting high conscientiousness and agreeableness. The advice must be practical (like troubleshooting electrical work) and avoid excessive emotional reaction, aligning with low extraversion. The discussion is framed around filtering and verification rather than broad condemnation.
